definition: Security model that assumes no user or device is trusted by default, even inside the network perimeter. Always verify.
Layered security approach using multiple controls so if one fails, others still protect the system.
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three core principles of information security.
Demilitarized Zone — a network segment that sits between the internal network and the internet, hosting public-facing services.
Gaining higher access rights than originally granted, either horizontally (same level) or vertically (admin level).
The part of malware or exploit code that performs the actual malicious action after delivery.
Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a standardized identifier for publicly known security vulnerabilities.
A shell session initiated by the target machine back to the attacker, bypassing inbound firewall rules.
Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — framework for controlling access and tracking user activity.
Multi-Factor Authentication — requires two or more verification factors: something you know, have, or are.
Security Information and Event Management — collects and analyzes security data from across the network in real time.
IDS detects and alerts on threats. IPS detects and actively blocks threats. IPS is inline, IDS is passive.
